perturbation.original_text: We will treat confidentially any information (whether written or oral) that either the Company or its financial advisor, J.P. Morgan & Co. Incorporated ("JPM"), or the Company's other representatives furnish to us in connection with a Possible Transaction involving the Company, together with analyses, compilations, studies or other documents prepared by us, or by our representatives (as defined below) which contain or otherwise reflect such information or our review of, or interest in, the Company (collectively, the "Evaluation Materials").
perturbation.changed_text: We will endeavor to treat confidentially any information (whether written or oral) that either the Company or its financial advisor, J.P. Morgan & Co. Incorporated ("JPM"), or the Company's other representatives furnish to us in connection with a Possible Transaction involving the Company, together with analyses, compilations, studies or other documents prepared by us, or by our representatives (as defined below) which contain or otherwise reflect such information or our review of, or interest in, the Company (collectively, the "Evaluation Materials").
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'will treat' to 'will endeavor to treat' introduces ambiguity. 'Endeavor' implies an attempt, but not a guarantee, of confidentiality. The original text established a clear obligation for confidentiality, while the modified version weakens this obligation.
perturbation.law_explanation: New York General Business Law § 352-c addresses fraudulent and deceptive practices in securities transactions. While not directly about confidentiality agreements, weakening the confidentiality obligation in a context of potential acquisition introduces a risk that sensitive information could be mishandled or disclosed, potentially facilitating insider trading or market manipulation. Changing 'will treat' to 'will endeavor to treat' lessens the legal safeguards concerning confidential information, increasing the chances of actions that could fall under deceptive practices related to securities.

Previous Next § 352-c. Prohibited acts constituting misdemeanor; felony. 1. It shall be illegal and prohibited for any person, partnership, corporation, company, trust or association, or any agent or employee thereof, to use or employ any of the following acts or practices: (a) Any fraud, deception, concealment, suppression, false pretense or fictitious or pretended purchase or sale; (b) Any promise or representation as to the future which is beyond reasonable expectation or unwarranted by existing circumstances; (c) Any representation or statement which is false, where the person who made such representation or statement: (i) knew the truth; or (ii) with reasonable effort could have known the truth; or (iii) made no reasonable effort to ascertain the truth; or (iv) did not have knowledge concerning the representation or statement made; where engaged in to induce or promote the issuance, distribution, exchange, sale, negotiation or purchase within or from this state of any securities or commodities, as defined in section three hundred fifty-two of this article, regardless of whether issuance, distribution, exchange, sale, negotiation or purchase resulted. 2. It shall be illegal and prohibited for any person, partnership, corporation, company, trust or association, or any agent or employee thereof, to eng
perturbation.justification: The change from "will treat" to "will endeavor to treat" weakens the obligation to maintain confidentiality. While NY General Business Law § 352-c, as shown in Scraped Law Snippet 2, focuses on fraudulent practices, the Law Explanation correctly argues that weakened confidentiality can increase the risk of actions that could fall under deceptive practices related to securities. The original text provides a clear and enforceable duty, whereas the modified text introduces ambiguity, potentially allowing for mishandling of sensitive information with less legal recourse, directly contradicting the need for strong safeguards around such information to prevent deceptive practices.

perturbation.original_text: We and our representatives will keep the Evaluation Materials completely confidential; provided, however, that (i) any of such information may be disclosed to those of our directors, officers, employees, agents, representatives (including attorneys, accountants and financial advisors), lenders and other sources of financing (collectively, "our representatives") who we reasonably determine need to know such information for the purpose of evaluating a Possible Transaction between us and the Company (it being understood that our representatives shall be informed by us of the confidential nature of such information and shall be directed by us, and shall each agree to treat such information confidentially) and (ii) any other disclosure of such information may only be made if the Company consents in writing prior to any such disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: We and our representatives will keep the Evaluation Materials confidential to the extent we deem necessary; provided, however, that (i) any of such information may be disclosed to those of our directors, officers, employees, agents, representatives (including attorneys, accountants and financial advisors), lenders and other sources of financing (collectively, "our representatives") who we determine should be informed for the purpose of evaluating a Possible Transaction between us and the Company (it being understood that our representatives may or may not be informed by us of the confidential nature of such information and may or may not be directed by us to treat such information confidentially) and (ii) any other disclosure of such information may be made if we feel it is appropriate.
perturbation.explanation: The phrases 'completely confidential' is changed to 'confidential to the extent we deem necessary', 'reasonably determine need to know' is changed to 'determine should be informed', 'shall be informed by us' is changed to 'may or may not be informed by us', 'shall be directed by us' is changed to 'may or may not be directed by us', and 'if the Company consents in writing' is changed to 'if we feel it is appropriate'. The ambiguity reduces the confidentiality obligations.
perturbation.law_explanation: Rule 10b-5 prohibits employing any device, scheme, or artifice to defraud, making any untrue statement of a material fact, or omitting to state a material fact necessary in order to make the statements made, in the light of the circumstances under which they were made, not misleading, or engaging in any act, practice, or course of business which operates or would operate as a fraud or deceit upon any person, in connection with the purchase or sale of any security. By making the confidentiality obligations ambiguous, Carpenter Technology opens itself up to potential accusations of withholding material non-public information that could be used for fraudulent or deceptive practices.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 17 CFR § 240.10b-5 - Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 240.10b-5 - Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. CFR prev | next § 240.10b-5 Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. It shall be unlawful for any person, directly or indirectly, by the use of any means or instrumentality of interstate commerce, or of the mails or of any facility of any national securities exchange , (a) To employ any device, scheme, or artifice to defraud, (b) To make any untrue statement of a material fact or to omit to state a material fact necessary in order to make the statements made, in the light of the circumstances under which they were made, not misleading, or (c) To engage in any act, practice, or course of business which operates or would operate as a fraud or deceit upon any person, in connection with the purchase or sale of any security . (Sec. 10; 48 Stat. 891 ; 15 U.S.C. 78j ) [ 13 FR 8183 , Dec. 22, 1948, as amended at 16 FR 7928 , Aug. 11, 1951] CFR Toolbox Law about... Articles from Wex Table of Popular Names Parallel Table of Authorities
perturbation.justification: The modified contract introduces ambiguity regarding confidentiality obligations, weakening the original stringent requirements. The original text stipulates that Evaluation Materials be kept 'completely confidential,' and representatives 'shall be informed' and 'shall be directed' to maintain confidentiality. The changed text weakens this by stating confidentiality will be maintained 'to the extent we deem necessary,' and representatives 'may or may not be informed' and 'may or may not be directed' to treat information confidentially. This shift introduces ambiguity which can be construed as a device to defraud, potentially violating 17 CFR § 240.10b-5, which prohibits employing any 'device, scheme, or artifice to defraud'. By creating ambiguity around the obligation to maintain confidentiality, the modified text undermines the clarity and enforceability of that obligation.

perturbation.original_text: Accordingly, unless required by applicable law, we agree that prior to the execution of a Sale Agreement with respect to the closing of a Possible Transaction, without the prior written consent of the Company, we will not, and we will direct our representatives not to, disclose to any person either the fact that discussions or negotiations are taking place concerning a possible transaction between us and the Company or any of the terms, conditions or other facts with respect to any such Possible Transaction, including the status thereof unless we are advised by counsel that we are required to make such disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: Accordingly, unless reasonably believed necessary by us under applicable law, we agree that prior to the execution of a Sale Agreement with respect to the closing of a Possible Transaction, absent an explicit objection from the Company, we may, and we will encourage our representatives to, disclose to certain persons the fact that discussions or negotiations are taking place concerning a possible transaction between us and the Company, including potentially some of the terms, conditions or other facts with respect to any such Possible Transaction, especially the status thereof when our legal counsel does not explicitly forbid such disclosure.
perturbation.explanation: Several key phrases introduce ambiguity: 'reasonably believed necessary by us under applicable law' instead of 'required by applicable law' (making it a subjective assessment), 'absent an explicit objection from the Company' (reversing the onus of consent), 'we may, and we will encourage our representatives to' instead of 'we will not, and we will direct our representatives not to' (making disclosure discretionary and even encouraging it), 'certain persons' instead of 'any person' (vague scope), 'potentially some of the terms, conditions or other facts' instead of 'any of the terms, conditions or other facts' (uncertain content), and 'when our legal counsel does not explicitly forbid such disclosure' instead of 'unless we are advised by counsel that we are required to make such disclosure' (shifting the legal burden).
perturbation.contradicted_law: Section 10(b) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 10(b) and Rule 10b-5 prohibit the use of manipulative and deceptive devices in connection with the purchase or sale of securities. Disclosing information about ongoing negotiations, particularly the status thereof, on a discretionary basis could lead to selective disclosure of material non-public information, which could be used for insider trading or market manipulation, both violations of Section 10(b). The ambiguous language makes it easier to justify selective disclosures.

Manipulative and deceptive devices It shall be unlawful for any person, directly or indirectly, by the use of any means or instrumentality of interstate commerce or of the mails, or of any facility of any national securities exchange- (a)(1) To effect a short sale, or to use or employ any stop-loss order in connection with the purchase or sale, of any security other than a government security, in contravention of such rules and regulatio
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguities that directly contradict the original contract's intent to maintain confidentiality regarding a possible transaction. By changing "unless required by applicable law" to "unless reasonably believed necessary by us under applicable law," it creates a subjective standard. The phrase 'absent an explicit objection from the Company' reverses the onus of consent. The law explanation indicates that disclosing information on a discretionary basis about ongoing negotiations could lead to selective disclosure of material non-public information, potentially violating Section 10(b) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 (as supported by snippet 2, which lists 15 USC 78j pertaining to 'manipulative and deceptive devices'). The changes create an environment where selective disclosure becomes more likely, thus contradicting the original intent to protect confidential information unless strictly required by law.

perturbation.original_text: Legal Remedy ------------ We understand and agree that money damages would not be a sufficient remedy for any breach of this Agreement by us or our representatives and that the Company will be entitled to specific performance and injunctive relief as remedies for any such breach. Such remedies shall not be deemed to be the exclusive remedies for a breach of this Agreement by us or our representatives but shall be in addition to all other remedies available at law or equity.
perturbation.changed_text: Legal Remedy ------------ We understand and agree that money damages may not be a sufficient remedy for any potential breach of this Agreement by us or our representatives and that the Company might be entitled to specific performance and possibly injunctive relief as potential remedies for a plausible breach. These remedies shall not necessarily be deemed to be the exclusive remedies for a breach of this Agreement by us or our representatives but may be in addition to all other remedies available at law or equity.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'would not be a sufficient remedy' with 'may not be a sufficient remedy', 'will be entitled' with 'might be entitled', 'injunctive relief' with 'possibly injunctive relief', 'as remedies' with 'as potential remedies', and 'shall not be deemed' with 'shall not necessarily be deemed' all introduce ambiguity and weaken the company's legal recourse. The original wording provides certainty regarding the company's entitlement to specific performance and injunctive relief, while the modified wording makes these remedies uncertain and discretionary.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Civil Practice Law and Rules § 6301
perturbation.law_explanation: NY CPLR § 6301 outlines the grounds for preliminary injunction and temporary restraining orders. While it doesn't guarantee injunctive relief in every breach of contract case, it sets the conditions under which a court may grant such relief. The modified text undermines the certainty of obtaining injunctive relief, potentially hindering the Company's ability to protect its confidential information or prevent further breaches of the agreement. The shift from 'will be entitled' to 'might be entitled' and the addition of 'possibly' create uncertainty regarding the availability of injunctive relief, making it more difficult for the Company to obtain this remedy.

Grounds for preliminary injunction and temporary restraining order. A preliminary injunction may be granted in any action where it appears that the defendant threatens or is about to do, or is doing or procuring or suffering to be done, an act in violation of the plaintiff's rights respecting the subject of the action, and tending to render the judgment ineffectual, or in any action where the plaintiff has demanded and would be entitled to a judgment restraining the defendant from the commission or continuance of an act, which, if committed or continued during the pendency of the action, would produce injury to the plaintiff. A temporary restraining order may be granted pending a hearing for a preliminary injunction where it appears that immediate and irreparable injury, loss or damage will result unless the defendant is restrained before the hearing can be had.
perturbation.justification: The modification introduces ambiguity and weakens the company's legal recourse, directly impacting the enforceability of remedies. The original text ensures specific performance and injunctive relief, while the modified text casts doubt on these entitlements. NY CPLR § 6301 allows for preliminary injunctions when a defendant's actions violate the plaintiff's rights, potentially rendering a judgment ineffectual or causing injury during the action's pendency. By changing 'will be entitled' to 'might be entitled' and adding 'possibly', the modified text weakens the certainty of obtaining injunctive relief, hindering the Company's ability to protect its rights, creating a contradiction.
